text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY OUTPATIENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Induction Assessment and Transplant Planning

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hematology/Oncology
SETTING: Inpatient Hematology/Oncology Unit (day of discharge)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female with newly diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML, NOS (ELN 2022 Adverse risk), presenting today for post-induction response assessment and discharge planning following a single cycle of 7+3 induction chemotherapy with midostaurin. She has achieved morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by flow cytometry on day +28 bone marrow biopsy and is now preparing for discharge with plans for allogeneic stem cell transplant.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old non-smoking female high school biology teacher with a prior history of hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) who was admitted approximately five weeks ago with a three-week history of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, gum bleeding, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. At presentation, her CBC showed a WBC of 87,400/uL with approximately 40% circulating blasts, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, and platelets 32,000/uL. Gingival hyperplasia with spontaneous oozing was identified on examination, and the spleen was palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin. Bone marrow biopsy confirmed hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 72% myeloblasts and Auer rods. Immunophenotype was consistent with AML (CD34+, CD117+, CD13+, CD33+, MPO+, HLA-DR+). Conventional karyotype showed 46,XX (normal). Comprehensive NGS revealed a FLT3-ITD (exon 14, 33 bp insertion, allelic ratio 0.68 — high), co-occurring with pathogenic mutations in DNMT3A (R882H, VAF 48%), RUNX1 (p.S291fs, VAF 44%), and subclonal TET2 (Q916*, VAF 22%) and WT1 (VAF 19%) mutations. FLT3-TKD, NPM1, IDH1/IDH2, CEBPA, TP53, and ASXL1 were all negative. ELN 2022 classification: Adverse risk (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio in NPM1-unmutated, normal karyotype AML, compounded by RUNX1 and DNMT3A co-mutations).

She was initiated on induction chemotherapy per the RATIFY protocol: cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day by continuous infusion on days 1-7, daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 2, and 3 (7+3 backbone), with midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily added on days 8-21. The induction course was complicated by:

1. Febrile neutropenia (grade 3) at approximately day +10, with temperature 38.9 degrees C and ANC of zero. CT chest showed bilateral lower lobe consolidations concerning for pneumonia. She was treated empirically with cefepime 2g IV q8h, with micafungin added for antifungal coverage. Blood cultures were negative. She defervesced after 5 days and completed a 14-day antibiotic course. No fungal infection confirmed but etiology was presumed to include possible early pulmonary fungal process.

2. Grade 2 nausea and vomiting attributed to midostaurin (appearing at approximately day +14), which was managed with the addition of prochlorperazine. Midostaurin was continued at full dose. A transient grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT 58, AST 45) was noted and resolved without intervention.

3. Severe mucositis was NOT a dominant feature during induction (it became more prominent during subsequent transplant conditioning), though she required significant transfusion support throughout her inpatient stay: 6 units packed red blood cells and 12 units of platelets.

Day +28 bone marrow biopsy (see pathology report) demonstrated a hypocellular marrow (10-15% cellularity) with emerging trilineage hematopoiesis, myeloblasts at 3% of total nucleated cells (meeting CR criteria of <5%), no Auer rods, and MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry (below 0.01% threshold). Conventional cytogenetics showed 46,XX. FLT3-ITD by PCR and DNMT3A/RUNX1 by sensitive sequencing were all undetected. This represents a morphologic and molecular complete remission after a single cycle of induction chemotherapy — an excellent early response.

Ms. Chen's counts have been recovering over the last ten days. Today, CBC shows WBC 3.1 k/uL, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelets 88,000/uL. She is no longer transfusion-dependent. She is afebrile, off antibiotics, tolerating a regular diet, and ambulating independently. She reports persistent mild fatigue, which is expected at this point in her recovery. She has no active complaints of bleeding, fever, or pain. Mucositis has resolved. She reports improved energy compared to her nadir.

Regarding transplant planning: HLA typing has been completed on Ms. Chen and her one full biological sibling. The sibling was found to be a 7/10 HLA match, which is not suitable for a myeloablative matched-related donor transplant given the risk profile. An unrelated donor search has been running concurrently through the National Marrow Donor Program (NMDP/Be The Match), and a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) has been identified — a 32-year-old CMV-seropositive male. The transplant center has confirmed availability and willingness to proceed. Given the adverse ELN risk classification and the achievement of first CR with MRD negativity, the team is in strong agreement that allogeneic SCT in CR1 represents the optimal consolidation strategy and only curative-intent approach. Delay to transplant should be minimized to reduce relapse risk.

No bridging consolidation chemotherapy is planned prior to transplant. The timeline is being compressed to target transplant within 6-8 weeks from today. Midostaurin maintenance as a bridge therapy during the pre-transplant interval is being considered and will be discussed with the transplant team at the upcoming evaluation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Persistent mild fatigue, improving. No fever, no night sweats. Appetite gradually returning. Weight stable since nadir.
- HEENT: Gingival bleeding resolved. No oral ulcers. No blurry vision.
- Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with walking more than two flights of stairs. No cough.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no peripheral edema.
- GI: Appetite improving. Nausea resolved. Normal stool consistency. No abdominal pain or distension. No blood per rectum.
- GU: No dysuria, no hematuria.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild bone pain in bilateral hips and lower back — likely related to marrow recovery. No joint swelling.
- Neurological: No headaches, no focal deficits, no confusion.
- Dermatologic: Petechiae resolved. No new rash or skin changes.
- Hematologic: No active bleeding. Bruising resolved.
- Endocrine: Continues levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. No new symptoms.
- Psychiatric: Anxious about the upcoming transplant but engaged and coping with support from her husband.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8 degrees C, blood pressure 114/72 mmHg, heart rate 84 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight 63.4 kg (down from 65.2 kg at admission; 68 kg prior to illness onset).

General: Appears comfortable at rest. Well-groomed, conversational. No acute distress. Pale but improving compared to prior examinations.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Conjunctivae pale but no active icteric sclera. Oral cavity: gingival tissue appears normal, no hyperplasia, no active bleeding, no ulcers. Mucous membranes moist.

Neck: Supple. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. Liver palpable approximately 1 cm below the right costal margin (previously 17.1 cm, now normalizing). Spleen: no longer palpable below the costal margin (previously 4 cm palpable; imaging at diagnosis showed 14.2 cm). No ascites.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No petechiae or bruising. Central venous catheter (right internal jugular tunneled catheter) site without erythema, induration, or discharge.

Neurological: Alert and oriented times three. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal neurological deficits. Gait steady.

Skin: No active rashes. Resolution of prior petechiae on bilateral lower extremities. No pallor of nails.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or femoral lymphadenopathy appreciated.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 3.1 k/uL (ANC estimated 1,550/uL)
- Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL (last transfusion was 12 days ago)
- Platelets: 88,000/uL (last platelet transfusion was 9 days ago)
- MCV: 90 fL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 102 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (eGFR >90)
- Glucose 98 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT 22 U/L (normalized), AST 18 U/L (normalized)
- Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL, Albumin 3.4 g/dL
- LDH 182 U/L (within normal limits — near-normalized from elevated at presentation)

Other:
- Uric acid 4.2 mg/dL (normal; allopurinol discontinued)
- Magnesium 1.8 mg/dL
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (within normal range on current levothyroxine dose)
- CMV IgG: positive (patient is CMV-seropositive, as noted)
- CMV PCR: sent (result pending at time of note)
- Blood cultures: no growth (final)

MRD Assessment (from day +28 bone marrow biopsy, reviewed today):
- Flow cytometry MRD: NEGATIVE (below 0.01% sensitivity threshold)
- FLT3-ITD by PCR: not detected
- Bone marrow blast count: 3% (morphologic CR)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
The pre-treatment CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (at diagnosis) showed mild splenomegaly (14.2 cm), hepatomegaly (17.1 cm), small bilateral pleural effusions, and no discrete masses or adenopathy. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative for CNS involvement.

No new imaging was obtained today. Repeat imaging will be performed as part of the pre-transplant evaluation at the transplant center, including a PET-CT for restaging and assessment of extramedullary disease prior to conditioning.

---

ASSESSMENT:

Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old female with de novo AML, NOS, normal karyotype, FLT3-ITD-positive (allelic ratio 0.68, high), with co-mutations in DNMT3A, RUNX1, and subclonal TET2 — ELN 2022 Adverse risk — who has achieved a morphologic and molecular complete remission with MRD negativity after a single cycle of 7+3 induction chemotherapy with midostaurin. This is a favorable early response in the context of high-risk disease biology.

1. AML, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse risk — morphologic CR, MRD negative (flow cytometry and PCR), after single induction cycle with 7+3 + midostaurin. CBC recovering (WBC 3.1, Hgb 9.8, Plt 88k). No longer transfusion-dependent. Excellent response, though given the mutational profile (FLT3-ITD high, DNMT3A, RUNX1 co-mutations), the risk of relapse without consolidative transplant remains very high. The graft-versus-leukemia (GvL) effect offered by allogeneic SCT is critical for disease control in this setting.

2. Allogeneic SCT planning — 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor (MUD) identified. Patient medically fit (ECOG PS 1, improving; LVEF 62% pre-induction, cardiac re-evaluation to be performed at transplant center; renal and hepatic function normal). Transplant center has been engaged. The plan is to proceed with myeloablative conditioning and MUD allogeneic HSCT as the definitive consolidation strategy. No consolidation chemotherapy is planned given the urgent timeline and the ability to proceed directly to transplant within the 6-8 week window.

3. Febrile neutropenia / induction-related infections — resolved. Completed 14-day antibiotic course for presumed pneumonia. Currently afebrile, off antibiotics. Pulmonary infiltrates noted on CT during the admission; lung reassessment to be included in pre-transplant staging CT/PET.

4. Hypothyroidism — chronic, stable. Continuing levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH within normal range.

5. Anemia of bone marrow recovery — hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, improving. No active bleeding. Transfusion threshold maintained at Hgb <7-8 g/dL outpatient, or sooner if symptomatic. Iron studies to be checked at next outpatient visit.

6. Psychosocial — patient is anxious about the upcoming transplant. She and her husband have been meeting with our transplant social worker. Husband is committed primary caregiver. Patient has requested information about prognosis and quality of life outcomes post-transplant, and a detailed counseling session was conducted today.

---

PLAN:

1. DISCHARGE TODAY from inpatient unit. Patient will be followed in the outpatient hematology/oncology clinic.

2. Transplant center consultation appointment scheduled within the next 10-14 days. Pre-transplant evaluation will include: repeat LVEF (echocardiogram), PFTs, PET-CT restaging, dental evaluation, infectious disease clearance, psychosocial evaluation, and formal consent for MUD alloHSCT.

3. FLT3-targeted maintenance bridging: midostaurin 50 mg PO BID is being considered as a bridge to transplant to maintain remission during the 6-8 week pre-transplant interval. Will discuss with transplant center whether to continue midostaurin during the pre-transplant window; if initiated, it will be monitored for GI tolerability. Decision deferred to joint discussion with the transplant team at the time of evaluation.

4. Monitoring at outpatient visits (every 1-2 weeks until transplant conditioning):
   - CBC with differential
   - Comprehensive metabolic panel
   - CMV PCR (patient is CMV-seropositive; donor is CMV-seropositive)
   - EBV PCR
   - Transfusion as clinically indicated (pRBC threshold Hgb <7-8 g/dL or symptomatic; platelets <10,000/uL or bleeding)

5. Infection prophylaxis during pre-transplant outpatient period:
   - Fluconazole 200 mg daily (antifungal prophylaxis while counts are recovering)
   - Acyclovir 400 mg BID (viral prophylaxis)
   - TMP-SMX (double strength, once daily, Monday/Wednesday/Friday) for PJP prophylaxis
   - No allopurinol (uric acid normalized)

6. Activity: as tolerated. Encourage light ambulation and progressive activity. No heavy lifting or contact sports given thrombocytopenia (platelets 88k).

7. Diet: regular diet, low-microbial precautions maintained during the early recovery period (avoid raw/undercooked meats, unwashed produce).

8. Medications at discharge:
   - Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism)
   - Fluconazole 200 mg PO daily
   - Acyclovir 400 mg PO BID
   - TMP-SMX DS one tablet PO MWF
   - Ondansetron 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea
   - Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea
   - Oxycodone 5 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough pain (limited supply for any residual mucositis-related discomfort; to be reassessed)

9. Return to clinic in 7-10 days for CBC check and clinical assessment. Sooner if febrile (temperature >38.0 degrees C), bleeding, new symptoms.

10. Patient and husband counseled extensively regarding:
    - Signs and symptoms of relapse (recurrent cytopenias, fatigue, bleeding, fever, gingival swelling)
    - Return precautions: fever, bleeding, new neurological symptoms
    - The transplant process and timeline
    - Long-term outlook: with MRD-negative CR1 and a well-matched donor, the probability of long-term disease-free survival with transplant is estimated at 40-55% given the adverse risk profile, though specific numbers were presented within confidence intervals and with appropriate uncertainty
    - Late effects of transplant (GVHD, infection risk, secondary malignancy, bone health, fertility)
    - Fertility: patient reports she and her husband are not interested in further fertility preservation at this stage (they have two children); prior discussion documented at initial consultation

11. HLA typing documentation: patient is confirmed 10/10 HLA-matched with the identified MUD. Donor availability confirmed with NMDP. Donor workup to proceed in parallel with recipient pre-transplant evaluation.

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expresses readiness to proceed. She is grateful for the response to treatment and motivated to complete the curative-intent transplant. Husband present at this visit and similarly engaged and informed.

Time spent on this encounter including counseling: approximately 60 minutes.

---

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Hematology/Oncology
Blood and Marrow Transplantation Program
